In a small beautiful Indian village. a little girl Amrita leaned back against her favorite tree and rested. After the intense1 heat of the desert sun, it was cool and green in the forest grove. Sometimes, Amrita climbed her tree. Sometimes the wind swayed her and she felt herself to be the forest queen. Sometimes she talked to her tree, sharing her daydreams and her secrets, but today was so peaceful that she just sat there silently. Amrita closed her eyes and sighed with pleasure. She always felt peaceful in the company of trees. 1. intense: very strong; grove: CAAWK! She heard the shrill2 alarm call of the peacock, echoing a small group of trees through the forest. A shiver shot down Amrita's spine and she 2unpleasant sound. shrill: high-pitched and scrambled to her feet.There was chaos in the forest. With a flick3 of their tails, the gazelles were gone. The rabbits scattered. Soon Amrita heard the tramp4 of many heavy boots, the crack of branches snapping. Through the trees she saw men marching, each one carrying something. Amrita strained5 to see. Bright edges! Sharp flashes! They were carrying axes. Amrita got tense and angry at the same time.flick: to move quickly back and forth ? What does the reaction of the animals suggest tramp: walk heavilystrained: tense or forced. 	before Amrita fully understands the danger?"Cut down every tree you see," she heard the chief woodcutter shout. "The Maharajah needs plenty of wood." Amrita drew in her breath. They couldn't cut down the forest! Without these trees there'd be no fruit to eat, no leaves to feed the cows, no shelter from the sun.Above her, watching over her, Amrita's own tree stirred in the breeze. "Don’t worry. I won't let them hurt you," she said out loud. "I promise I'll protect you. I don't know how, but I will!"Quick as a blackbuck, she ran to the village. Amrita found her mother. "Amma, Amma," she panted, pushing windswept hair out of her eyes, "I saw men in the forest, men with axes, and they are going to cut down the Amrita's mother rushed around the village, calling the women away from their work. "We must save the trees!" she urged. "Come on!" They reached the forest to find the woodcutters sharpening their axes. Amrita's mother greeted the men politely, pressing her hands together and bowing her head: "Namaste. We do not want trouble, but we cannot let you cut down our trees."The chief woodcutter cast his eyes over the straggle6 of women before him and snorted. "You do not own these trees. We have orders from the Maharajah.""Sir, these trees are our life," Amrita's mother implored . "Their roots hold the soil together; they keep the land from sliding away during the monsoon rains. Without them our fields and homes will be washed away.""Never mind your mud huts," the chief replied with a sweep of his hand. "With this wood, the Maharajah will have the finest palace in all of India!""Please!" begged Amrita's mother. "These roots soak up rain, so the earth can give us spring water. Can't you see? We need these trees to survive.""Enough!" yelled the chief. "Now, get out of my way and let me work!" He turned to the woodcutters and ordered, "Cut down the trees!" A burly8 woodcutter strolled over to an ancient khejari with his axe. With a swift swoop, the iron blade of his axe bit deep into the bark.With a terrible groan, the mighty tree came crashing to the ground. Amrita's mother covered her mouth with her hands. Amrita stared in disbelief. The woodcutters began to chop at another tree, and another. Soon the grove was a graveyard of trees. Broken limbs scattered the floor. Leaves dropped like tears.A woodcutter brushed past Amrita, toward her own special tree. "No! No! Please don't!" she cried, tears springing to her eyes. "Please don't cut down my tree." The woodcutter advanced.Amrita stepped in front of him, blocking his path. Her voice shook as she spoke: "I will not let anyone harm my tree."The woodcutter laughed out loud. "Little girl, there's nothing you can do to stop us." Amrita thought of her beloved tree lying dead on the forest floor and she ran from the woodcutter toward her tree.The India BookmarkThe Bishnoi community of Rajasthan follows 29 principles laid down by Guru Jambheshwar in the 15th century. Several of these principles focus on protecting trees and wildlife. In 1730, Amrita Devi Bishnoi and more than 350 villagers sacrificed their lives to save Khejri trees in Khejarli village.Amrita flung her arms around her tree, pressing herself against it. "If you want to cut the tree, you will have to cut me first!" The man and his axe were behind her. She could hear the sound of her own breathing, hard and loud and fast. The man raised his "Swing your axe!" commanded the chief. Amrita clenched her teeth and clung hard onto her old friend, so that the bumpy10 bark was pressing into her cheek and arms. She felt the strength of the tree coursing through her. And she knew with a fierce bright certainty that she was doing the right thing.9. flung: threw something 	"Swing your 	with forceaxe!" the chief 10. bumpy: having many shouted again. small bumps on the surface"I... " the woodcutter faltered11. He looked down at the girl—this mere sapling of a girl12— her eyes squeezed shut, her thin arms hugging so tight, her tear stained cheeks pale with fright. "I... I cannot."     Amrita opened one eye, then another, to see the woodcutter's head bowed, his axe at his feet. All around her, people were hugging trees. Women and children, wives and daughters, grandmothers and toddlers all hugged the trees. Some ancient trees had such broad trunks that generations of women were joining hands to embrace them. The axes lay on the forest floor. The men huddled together and talked in low voices. Then, without a word, the laborers picked up their axes and walked out of the wood. Amrita's mother called her. "What were you thinking of? I was so afraid." "So was I," said Amrita. Her mother sat down beside her on the forest floor and stroked her hair. "You know the woodcutters will tell the Maharajah what happened," she said gently. "They will come back, or the Maharajah himself will come... "The next morning, the women were distracted at their work. Their hands were busy, but their eyes were anxiously wandering towards the horizon. Would the woodcutters return? Or the Maharajah? Would he punish those who had disobeyed him? That afternoon, amidst a thunder of hooves and a cloud of dust, the Do you think the Maharajah changed Maharajah arrived.The women joined hands and pulled their children close. But Amrita rose to greet the Maharajah with all the dignity of a forest queen. She was surprised to see that he carried not an axe but a bright bundle. He climbed down from his horse. Amrita watched in wonder as he unwrapped the silken cloth. "I present this royal decree to you, Amrita," said the Maharajah, "and to the women of your village, in honor of your courage and your wisdom. I promise that, from this day on, no tree in this forest will ever be cut down."  Do you think the Maharajah changed only because of Amrita’s bravery, or also because he understood the importance of trees? Give reasons.Hundreds of years later, folk songs of the people who hugged the trees still echo through the villages of India. Amrita's courage has inspired people across the land to stand together to protect forests. Thousands of trees have been saved, and a million more planted.Where did Amrita Devi live?Rajasthan 	(b) Punjab 	(c) Gujarat 	(d) MaharashtraWhich community did Amrita Devi belong to?Rajput 	(b) Bishnoi 	(c) Maratha 	(d) AhomWhy did the king’s men come to the village?To collect taxes 	(b) To build houses  To cut trees for the king’s palaceTo capture animalsWhat type of trees were the soldiers ordered to cut?Neem trees 	(b) banyan trees 	(c) Mango trees 	(d) Khejri treesWhat action did Amrita Devi take to protect the tree?She planted more trees 	(b) She stood in front of it   	(c) She hugged the tree   	(d) She hid the toolsFill in the Blanks: 	/Amrita loved to be in the company of __________.Amrita saw some people coming with _______ in their hands.The Maharajah ordered that no trees should be cut in ______ villages.Maharajah was ______ to see Amrita’s dedication towards the trees.The trees were important for the ______ of the village.Arrange the following events in the order  (1-7) in which they happened in the chapter. The woodcutters leave when they see the villagers protecting the trees. The Maharajah arrives the next day and promises that no tree will be cut again. Amrita hugs her favourite tree and refuses to let it be cut. The women of the village go to the forest and try to stop the men. Amrita runs to the village and tells her mother about the woodcutters. The Maharajah’s men come to the forest to cut down the trees. Answer the following questions: Did Amrita love trees? Give examples.What change came to animals’ behaviour suddenly and why?‘Amrita got tense and angry at the same time.’ Why?What promise did Amrita make to her tree?What did Amrita’s mother tell the woodcutter? What does it tell you about her?She felt the strength of the tree coursing through her. And she Support your knew with a fierce bright certainty that she was doing the right answers with thing. Elaborate the feelings of the little girl at that moment. examples from the story wherever How did Maharaja appreciate the courage and bravery of the 	possible.little girl?Amrita's courage has inspired people across the land to stand together to protect forests. Thousands of trees have been saved and a million more planted. Write a comprehensive note on the importance of forests in our life.	Put on Your Thinking Caps	 	/Write short character sketches of: Amrita 	• Amrita’s motherChief woodcutter 	• MaharajaApart from telling us the value of trees, the story also gives a message of women empowerment. Though weak and considered meek, women of the village did a task that even men wouldn’t be able to do. Write about some women who had made it great.	Dictionary & Vocabulary Skills	  	/Imagine the guide words at the top of a dictionary page are: forest — founder  	Circle the words that would appear on that page?  forest            forever            forbid            fortune            fossil           fountainEach sentence has one spelling error. Write the correctly spelled word in the given space. 	1. A sudden shril cry echoed through the silent forest. _____________________ 	2. The gazelles dissapeared in a flash of movement. _____________________ 	3. The woodcutter raised his ax with great confidance. _____________________ 	4. Amrita stood firm despite the danger that approched her. _____________________ 	5. The villagers gatherd together to protect the trees. _____________________ 	6. The man hesitated, his voice begining to falter. _____________________ 	7. Leaves droped slowly like tears from the broken branches. _____________________	Grammar Lab	  	/Grammar Power (Subject Verb Agreement)In sentences, subjects and verbs must agree in number.When the subject is singular, the verb must be singular too. 	 Example: He walks to school every day.If the subject is plural, the verb must also be plural. 	 Example: They walk to school every day.When the subject of the sentence has two or more nouns or pronouns connected by and, the verb must be plural. 	 Example: The students and the teachers wait for the result anxiously.When a phrase comes between the subject and the verb, the verb still agrees with the subject. 	 Example: The student, as well as the teachers, is excited.When two or more singular nouns or pronouns are connected by "or" or "nor," use a singular verb. 	 Example: The Chairman or the Principal gives award to the students.The words "each," "each one," "either," "neither," "everyone," "everybody," "anyone," "anybody," "nobody," "somebody," "someone," and "no one" are singular and require a singular verb. 	 Example:Each of the participants was given a participation certificate.Neither solution is correct.Uncountable nouns take a singular verb. 	 Example:Education is the key to success.Honesty is the best policy.Collective nouns are considered singular and take a singular verb. 	 Example: The herd is grazing in the field.Identify and circle the part of the sentence which has an error.1. The team havea new coach.ABC2.Neither of the people have the courageto speak the truth.ABC3.I don’t has        energy     to do anything now.ABC4.Most of the      sweets    was tasty.ABC5.The books that  were lying on the tablewas mine.ABC6.My group          of friendsare very dear to me.ABCFirst identify the subject, then choose the correct verb.ConnectCommunicate	Listening Skills	 	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingListen carefully as your teacher reads the passage. Imagine the scene and complete the forest map below. Write the details you hear in the correct places.Living Things in the ForestSounds HeardDangers Mentioned____________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________	Speaking Skills	 	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingImagine yourself to be Amrita’s favourite tree. You thank her for her brave act. Create and present a short conversation in which the tree thanks Amrita for her bravery, and Amrita explains why she protected the tree. Use expressions and simple actions while speaking.	Writing Skills	  	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingYou came across the following picture. You feel sad to see the condition of earth where trees are cut down thoughtlessly. Write an article on the topic ‘Grow More Trees’.________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________CompetenciesBeyondCG:2, C:2.2	 Interconnected Learning with Maths 	Critical ThinkingA forest has 500 trees. Each year, 8% of trees are cut.How many trees will remain after 1 year?Will more than half remain after 5 years? Yes/No SEL Corner  Decision Making 	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical Thinking ῀Amrita did not stay silent when she saw danger in the forest. What does this teach you about taking responsibility in difficult situations?Have you ever stood up for something you believed was right? What happened, and how did you feel?	 Explore 	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingScan & LearnScan the QR code to access Ask questions, clear yout the worksheet for this doubts, and learn more with chapter.AI.Collect information and pictures about the ‘Chipko Movement’ started by the Bishnoi Community and the people, especially women, associated with it and make a project file about it.Listening text for the teacher on page ????.
